mn8:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn8:10.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, by completely going beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking, ‘There is nothing,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness.
mn8:10.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:10.5: These are called peaceful dwellings in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:11.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, by completely going beyond the dimension of nothingness, enters and dwells in the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā*.
mn8:11.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:11.5: These are called peaceful dwellings in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:12.1: “But here, Cunda, effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.10: ‘Others will be covetous; here we will be non-covetous’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.11: ‘Others will have minds of ill will [*byāpannacittā*]; here we will have minds free of ill will [*abyāpannacittā*]’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.12: ‘Others will have wrong view; here we will have right view’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.13: ‘Others will have wrong intention; here we will have right intention’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.14: ‘Others will have wrong speech; here we will have right speech’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.15: ‘Others will have wrong action; here we will have right action’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.16: ‘Others will have wrong livelihood; here we will have right livelihood’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.17: ‘Others will have wrong effort; here we will have right effort’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.18: ‘Others will have wrong *sati*; here we will have right *sati*’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.19: ‘Others will have wrong *samādhi*; here we will have right *samādhi*’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.2: ‘Others will be harmful; here we will be non-harming’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.20: ‘Others will have wrong knowledge; here we will have right knowledge’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.21: ‘Others will have wrong liberation; here we will have right liberation’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.22: ‘Others will be overcome by sloth and drowsiness; here we will be free of sloth and drowsiness’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.23: ‘Others will be agitated; here we will be unagitated’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.24: ‘Others will be doubtful; here we will have crossed over doubt’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.25: ‘Others will be angry; here we will be non-angry’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.26: ‘Others will be resentful; here we will be non-resentful’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.27: ‘Others will be contemptuous; here we will be non-contemptuous’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.28: ‘Others will be insolent; here we will be non-insolent’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.29: ‘Others will be envious; here we will be non-envious’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.3: ‘Others will kill living beings; here we will refrain from killing living beings’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.30: ‘Others will be stingy; here we will be non-stingy’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.31: ‘Others will be deceitful; here we will be non-deceitful’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.32: ‘Others will be crafty; here we will be non-crafty’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.33: ‘Others will be stubborn; here we will be non-stubborn’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.34: ‘Others will be conceited; here we will be non-conceited’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.35: ‘Others will be hard to admonish; here we will be easy to admonish’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.36: ‘Others will have bad friends; here we will have good friends’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.37: ‘Others will be negligent; here we will be diligent’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.38: ‘Others will be without faith; here we will have faith’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.39: ‘Others will be shameless; here we will have conscience’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.4: ‘Others will take what is not given; here we will refrain from taking what is not given’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.40: ‘Others will be without concern; here we will have concern’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.41: ‘Others will be little learned; here we will be learned’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.42: ‘Others will be lazy; here we will be energetic’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.43: ‘Others will have lost *sati* [*muṭṭhassatī*]; here we will have *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatī*]’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.44: ‘Others will be without discernment [*duppaññā*]; here we will be endowed with discernment [*paññāsampannā*]’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.45: ‘Others will cling to their own views, grasp at fixed positions, and be hard to relinquish; here we will not cling to our own views, not grasp at fixed positions, and be easy to relinquish’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.5: ‘Others will be unchaste; here we will be chaste’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.6: ‘Others will speak falsely; here we will refrain from false speech’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.7: ‘Others will speak divisively; here we will refrain from divisive speech’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.8: ‘Others will speak harshly; here we will refrain from harsh speech’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:12.9: ‘Others will engage in idle chatter; here we will refrain from idle chatter’—effacement is to be practiced by you.
mn8:13.0: 2. The Exposition on Arousing the Mind [*citta*]
mn8:13.1: “Even the arousing of the mind [*citta*], Cunda, I say is of great benefit with wholesome *dhammas*; how much more so when they are followed through by body and speech.
mn8:13.2: Therefore, Cunda, this mind [*citta*] should be aroused: ‘Others will be harmful; here we will be non-harming.’
mn8:13.3: ‘Others will kill living beings; here we will refrain from killing living beings’ …
mn8:13.4: ‘Others will cling to their own views, grasp at fixed positions, and be hard to relinquish; here we will not cling to our own views, not grasp at fixed positions, and be easy to relinquish’—this mind [*citta*] should be aroused.
mn8:14.1: “Suppose, Cunda, there were a rough road, and there were another smooth road for getting beyond it.
mn8:14.2: Or suppose, Cunda, there were a difficult ford, and there were another easy ford for getting beyond it.
mn8:14.3: In the same way, for a harmful person, non-harming is for getting beyond; for a person who kills living beings, refraining from killing living beings is for getting beyond; for a person who takes what is not given, refraining from taking what is not given is for getting beyond; for an unchaste person, refraining from unchastity is for getting beyond; for a person who speaks falsely, refraining from false speech is for getting beyond; for a person who speaks divisively, refraining from divisive speech is for getting beyond; for a person who speaks harshly, refraining from harsh speech is for getting beyond; for a person who engages in idle chatter, refraining from idle chatter is for getting beyond; for a covetous person, non-covetousness is for getting beyond; for a person with a mind [*citta*] of ill will, non-ill will is for getting beyond.
mn8:14.4: For a person with wrong view, right view is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong intention, right intention is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong speech, right speech is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong action, right action is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong livelihood, right livelihood is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong effort, right effort is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong *sati*, right *sati* is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong *samādhi*, right *samādhi* is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong knowledge, right knowledge is for getting beyond; for a person with wrong liberation, right liberation is for getting beyond.
mn8:14.5: For a person overcome by sloth and drowsiness, freedom from sloth and drowsiness is for getting beyond; for an agitated person, non-agitation is for getting beyond; for a doubtful person, having crossed over doubt is for getting beyond; for an angry person, non-anger is for getting beyond; for a resentful person, non-resentment is for getting beyond; for a contemptuous person, non-contempt is for getting beyond; for an insolent person, non-insolence is for getting beyond; for an envious person, non-envy is for getting beyond; for a stingy person, non-stinginess is for getting beyond; for a deceitful person, non-deceit is for getting beyond; for a crafty person, non-craftiness is for getting beyond; for a stubborn person, non-stubbornness is for getting beyond; for a conceited person, non-conceit is for getting beyond; for a person hard to admonish, being easy to admonish is for getting beyond; for a person with bad friends, having good friends is for getting beyond; for a negligent person, diligence is for getting beyond; for a person without faith, faith is for getting beyond; for a shameless person, conscience is for getting beyond; for a person without concern, concern is for getting beyond; for a little-learned person, much learning is for getting beyond; for a lazy person, arousing energy is for getting beyond; for a person who has lost *sati* [*muṭṭhassatissa*], having *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatitā*] is for getting beyond; for a person without discernment [*duppaññassa*], accomplishment in discernment [*paññāsampadā*] is for getting beyond; for a person who clings to his own views, grasps at fixed positions, and is hard to relinquish, not clinging to one’s own views, not grasping at fixed positions, and being easy to relinquish is for getting beyond.
mn8:15.0: 4. The Exposition on the Upward Course
mn8:15.1: “Suppose, Cunda, that whatever unwholesome *dhammas* there are, all of them lead downward; and whatever wholesome *dhammas* there are, all of them lead upward.
mn8:15.2: In the same way, for a harmful person, non-harming is for the upward course; for a person who kills living beings, refraining from killing living beings is for the upward course …
mn8:15.3: for a person who clings to his own views, grasps at fixed positions, and is hard to relinquish, not clinging to one’s own views, not grasping at fixed positions, and being easy to relinquish is for the upward course.
mn8:16.1: “Now, Cunda, that someone who is himself mired in the mire should pull out another who is mired in the mire—this is not possible.
mn8:16.10: For a person who speaks harshly, refraining from harsh speech is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.11: For a person who engages in idle chatter, refraining from idle chatter is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.12: For a covetous person, non-covetousness is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.13: For a person with a mind [*citta*] of ill will, non-ill will is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.14: For a person with wrong view, right view is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.15: For a person with wrong intention, right intention is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.16: For a person with wrong speech, right speech is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.17: For a person with wrong action, right action is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.18: For a person with wrong livelihood, right livelihood is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.19: For a person with wrong effort, right effort is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.2: But that someone who is himself not mired in the mire should pull out another who is mired in the mire—this is possible.
mn8:16.20: For a person with wrong *sati*, right *sati* is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.21: For a person with wrong *samādhi*, right *samādhi* is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.22: For a person with wrong knowledge, right knowledge is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.23: For a person with wrong liberation, right liberation is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.24: For a person overcome by sloth and drowsiness, freedom from sloth and drowsiness is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.25: For an agitated person, non-agitation is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.26: For a doubtful person, having crossed over doubt is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.27: For an angry person, non-anger is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.28: For a resentful person, non-resentment is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.29: For a contemptuous person, non-contempt is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.3: That someone who is himself untamed, untrained, and not fully extinguished should tame, train, and bring another to full extinguishment—this is not possible.
mn8:16.30: For an insolent person, non-insolence is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.31: For an envious person, non-envy is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.32: For a stingy person, non-stinginess is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.33: For a deceitful person, non-deceit is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.34: For a crafty person, non-craftiness is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.35: For a stubborn person, non-stubbornness is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.36: For a conceited person, non-conceit is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.37: For a person hard to admonish, being easy to admonish is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.38: For a person with bad friends, having good friends is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.39: For a negligent person, diligence is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.4: But that someone who is himself tamed, trained, and fully extinguished should tame, train, and bring another to full extinguishment—this is possible.
mn8:16.40: For a person without faith, faith is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.41: For a shameless person, conscience is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.42: For a person without concern, concern is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.43: For a little-learned person, much learning is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.44: For a lazy person, arousing energy is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.45: For a person who has lost *sati* [*muṭṭhassatissa*], having *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatitā*] is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.46: For a person without discernment [*duppaññassa*], accomplishment in discernment [*paññāsampadā*] is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.47: For a person who clings to his own views, grasps at fixed positions, and is hard to relinquish, not clinging to one’s own views, not grasping at fixed positions, and being easy to relinquish is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.5: In the same way, for a harmful person, non-harming is for full extinguishment; for a person who kills living beings, refraining from killing living beings is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.6: For a person who takes what is not given, refraining from taking what is not given is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.7: For an unchaste person, refraining from unchastity is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.8: For a person who speaks falsely, refraining from false speech is for full extinguishment.
mn8:16.9: For a person who speaks divisively, refraining from divisive speech is for full extinguishment.
mn8:17.1: “So, Cunda, the exposition on effacement has been taught by me, the exposition on arousing the mind [*citta*] has been taught, the exposition on getting beyond has been taught, the exposition on the upward course has been taught, the exposition on final *nibbāna* has been taught.
mn8:17.10: The eighth, the Effacement sutta, is finished.
mn8:17.2: What should be done by a teacher for his disciples, seeking their welfare, compassionate, out of compassion—that I have done for you.
mn8:17.3: These are the roots of trees, Cunda; these are empty huts. Meditate [*jhāyatha*], Cunda; do not be negligent; do not later be remorseful. This is our instruction.”
mn8:17.5: The Venerable Mahācunda was pleased and approved of what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn8:2.1: Then the Venerable Mahācunda, in the evening, having emerged from seclusion, went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the Venerable Mahācunda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn8:3.1: “Bhante, these various kinds of views that arise in the world—
mn8:3.2: whether connected with views about self or connected with views about the world—
mn8:3.3: for a *bhikkhu* attending to them, right from the beginning, is there thus an abandoning of these views, is there thus a relinquishment of these views?”
mn8:3.4: “Cunda, these various kinds of views that arise in the world—
mn8:3.5: whether connected with views about self or connected with views about the world—
mn8:3.6: where these views arise, where they remain as underlying tendencies [*anusenti*], and where they become active: seeing that as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññā*] thus, ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self,’ there is thus an abandoning of these views, there is thus a relinquishment of these views.
mn8:4.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn8:4.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:4.5: These are called pleasant dwellings in this very life in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:5.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, with the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, which has internal clarity and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind [*cetaso*], without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn8:5.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:5.5: These are called pleasant dwellings in this very life in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:6.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, with the fading away of *pīti*, dwells in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*], and experiences *sukha* with the body, entering and dwelling in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘Equanimous [*upekkhā*], with *sati*, one dwells pleasantly.’
mn8:6.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:6.5: These are called pleasant dwellings in this very life in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:7.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and grief, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, with purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati*.
mn8:7.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:7.5: These are called pleasant dwellings in this very life in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:8.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, by completely going beyond *rūpasaññās*, with the disappearance of *paṭighasaññās*, through non-attention to *nānattasaññās*, thinking, ‘Space is infinite,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space.
mn8:8.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:8.5: These are called peaceful dwellings in the noble *vinaya*.
mn8:9.1: “Now, Cunda, it is possible that here some *bhikkhu*, by completely going beyond the dimension of infinite space, thinking, ‘Consciousness [*viññāṇa*] is infinite,’ enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*].
mn8:9.4: But these are not called effacements in the noble *vinaya*, Cunda.
mn8:9.5: These are called peaceful dwellings in the noble *vinaya*.
